channel.title: NYT > World News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Iran War Live Updates: Tehran Says It Is Reviewing U.S. Proposal as Trump Makes New Threats
item.description: An Iranian official said his government was looking at an American plan to end the war, hours after President Trump threatened to resume strikes. Earlier Wednesday, another Iranian official dismissed a reported proposal as a “list of American wishes.”
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times

item.title: 3 People Are Evacuated From Cruise Ship With Hantavirus Outbreak
item.description: Three people with possible symptoms of the disease were evacuated on Wednesday morning from the Dutch-flagged MV Hondius.
item.dc:creator: Claire Moses and Aimee Ortiz
item.media:description: The MV Hondius off the port of Praia, the capital of Cape Verde, on Wednesday.

item.title: World Cup Dynamic Ticket Pricing Drives Argentine Soccer Fans To Extremes
item.description: Dynamic ticket pricing has made this year’s tournament the most expensive in history, pushing Argentine fans to extremes.
item.dc:creator: Tariq Panja, Emma Bubola, Lucía Cholakian Herrera and Sarah Pabst
item.media:description: River Plate soccer fans at Estadio Monumental stadium in Buenos Aires, at the biggest club game in Argentina against rival Boca Juniors last month.

item.title: Iran’s Oil Sector and Economy Are Under Pressure as U.S. Blockade Bites
item.description: Tehran says it can endure the pain, but its oil may soon have nowhere to go.
item.dc:creator: Farnaz Fassihi
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Fatemeh Bahrami/Anadolu Agency, via Getty Images
item.media:description: With the United States blockading Iranian shipping, Iran risks running out of room to store oil in places like Kharg Island.

item.title: Reclaiming the Name of the Black Hero Who Inspired ‘Uncle Tom’s Cabin’
item.description: After 42 years of slavery, Josiah Henson escaped to Canada, where he wrote a memoir, founded a school and led others to freedom. But his home long bore the name “Uncle Tom,” to the offense of many.
item.dc:creator: Ian Austen and Ian Willms
item.media:credit: Ian Willms for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Henson House at the Josiah Henson Museum of African-Canadian History in Dresden, Canada.

item.title: Leo XIV Is the First Pope From the U.S. He’s Making That an Asset.
item.description: A year into his papacy, Leo has shown a readiness to challenge Washington, defying any concerns about the election of an American pontiff.
item.dc:creator: Motoko Rich and Elizabeth Dias
item.media:description: Pope Leo XIV arriving at St. Peter’s Square in the Vatican on Wednesday.

item.title: A Hotel in Venezuela’s Capital Has Become the U.S. Embassy’s De Facto Headquarters
item.description: The J.W. Marriott in Venezuela’s capital is suddenly awash in U.S. diplomats, spies and fortune seekers, showcasing changes in the country.
item.dc:creator: Simon Romero and Todd Heisler
item.media:description: The Marriott’s top floor serves as the de facto headquarters of the U.S. Embassy in Caracas.

item.title: In Talks With Iran, China Calls for Opening of Strait of Hormuz
item.description: China’s top diplomat also warned against the restarting of hostilities as he met with Iran’s foreign minister. The U.S. has urged China to pressure Iran to reopen the waterway.
item.dc:creator: Chris Buckley
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Seyed Abbas Araqchi, via Telegram, via Reuters
item.media:description: Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi of Iran meeting with Foreign Minister Wang Yi of China in Beijing on Wednesday.

item.title: In Romania, Living With Weapons of War Spilling Into NATO Territory
item.description: Four years of battle in Ukraine is taking a toll on neighboring Romanians. Even if the war has not crossed the border, drones have done so.
item.dc:creator: Lara Jakes and Andrada Lautaru
item.media:credit: Andreea Campeanu for The New York Times

item.title: Japan’s Takaichi Pitches Strength and Stability in Visits to Australia and Vietnam
item.description: Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi is attempting to raise Japan’s profile at a time when China’s clout is rising and doubts about the United States are spreading.
item.dc:creator: Javier C. Hernández
item.category: Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
item.media:description: Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi of Japan arriving in Canberra, Australia, on Sunday.

item.title: In Venezuela, Trump Vowed to Show Accountability. But Secret Oil Deals Linger.
item.description: Trump officials and their Venezuelan allies have promised a new era of accountability to unlock Venezuela’s immense oil wealth. But the country’s oil industry remains a black hole.
item.dc:creator: Anatoly Kurmanaev and Adriana Loureiro Fernandez
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Cardon Refinery in Punto Fijo, Falcon, Venezuela. Venezuela’s oil industry is undergoing rapid change under Delcy Rodríguez’s interim presidency, as it shifts toward attracting private investment.

item.title: Trump Looks for a Silver Bullet to End the Iran War. There May Be None.
item.description: The president is trying to ratchet up the economic pressure on Tehran, but Iran’s government is unlikely to make a deal without a big, face-saving compromise.
item.dc:creator: Steven Erlanger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: A billboard in Tehran last week, depicting a closed Strait of Hormuz over the mouth of President Trump.

item.title: Rare Comet Appears Over New Zealand, Australia and South Africa
item.description: Known as C/2025 R3 PANSTARRS, the comet will be visible to stargazers using a telescope in New Zealand, Australia and South Africa.
item.dc:creator: Laura Chung

item.title: Modi’s Triumph in West Bengal Elections Puts Him Closer to an Opposition-Free India
item.description: With his triumph in West Bengal state elections, Prime Minister Narendra Modi has moved closer to his dream of an opposition-free India.
item.dc:creator: Alex Travelli, Hari Kumar and Pragati K.B.
item.media:description: Supporters of Mr. Modi’s Bharatiya Janata Party celebrating in Kolkata as the party headed to victory in the West Bengal state assembly elections on Monday.

item.title: Macron Wants U.S. and Iran to Open Strait While They Negotiate
item.description: Emmanuel Macron, the French president, hopes the Strait of Hormuz can reopen even before the U.S.-Iran war formally ends. His plan has many pitfalls.
item.dc:creator: Mark Landler
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: President Emmanuel Macron of France has shown frustration about the economic costs of the blocked strait.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Leo Sands, Qasim Nauman and Erica L. Green

item.title: Ted Turner, Creator of CNN and the 24-Hour News Cycle, Dies at 87
item.description: As one of the most important figures in media history, he oversaw a vast cable empire of news, sports and entertainment channels.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Kandell
item.media:description: Ted Turner was a visionary media mogul who dominated the cable television industry, and extended his reach into movies, major league baseball and even yachting.

item.title: Why Is Gas So Much More Expensive in Some States Than Others?
item.description: Shipping, taxes, competition and environmental regulations all play a part in the cost of gasoline. That can lead to stark differences in pump prices.
item.dc:creator: Emmett Lindner
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: The national average gas price has soared about 50 percent since the start of the war with Iran.

item.title: Trump’s U-turn and hopes of a deal ease tensions in energy markets.
item.dc:creator: Qasim Nauman and Erica L. Green
item.media:description: A driver pumped fuel at a gas station in Gresham, Ore., in April.

item.title: Khaled Sabsabi’s Rocky Road From Australia to the Biennale
item.description: The Lebanese-Australian artist almost lost his Venice Biennale commission after he was accused of supporting terrorism. Reinstated, he is arriving in Venice showing not one but two works.
item.dc:creator: Will Higginbotham
item.media:credit: Matteo de Mayda for The New York Times
item.media:description: Khaled Sabsabi at his exhibition at the Australia Pavilion of the Venice Biennale, in April.

item.title: Argentine Fans Are Struggling to Afford This World Cup
item.description: Argentine fans have flocked to previous World Cups by the tens of thousands, but the high cost of attending this year’s event is creating a new challenge. Tariq Panja, a global sports correspondent for The New York Times, traveled to Buenos Aires to meet fans who were reckoning with the rising prices, which FIFA said would create income to help fund soccer development around the world.
item.dc:creator: Tariq Panja, Leila Medina and Sarah Pabst

item.title: Trump and Rubio Insist Iran War Is Over, Even as Missiles Fly During Cease-Fire
item.description: The White House is turning to rhetorical leaps as President Trump tries to put the biggest political crisis of his presidency behind him.
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Despite Secretary of State Marco Rubio’s declaration that the objectives of the war have been accomplished, they clearly have not.

item.title: Hantavirus Outbreaks Are Rare, but They Aren’t Going Away and There’s No Cure
item.description: Since the family of rodent-borne infections were identified in the 1950s, they have turned up all over the world.
item.dc:creator: Adeel Hassan
item.media:description: A cruise ship remained stranded off the coast of Cape Verde after a suspected hantavirus outbreak on the vessel killed at least three people and left several others ill.

item.title: Deadly Russian Strikes Rip Into Ukrainian Cities
item.description: At least 12 people were killed in Zaporizhzhia, one of the highest tolls from a single attack so far this year. The cities of Dnipro and Kramatorsk were also attacked.
item.dc:creator: Cassandra Vinograd
item.media:description: The site of a Russian airstrike in Zaporizhzhia, Ukraine, on Tuesday. The death toll in Zaporizhzhia was one of the highest from a single attack so far this year.

item.title: Louise Arbour Named as Canada’s Governor General
item.description: Prime Minister Mark Carney’s appointment of Louise Arbour, a former Supreme Court justice, as governor general, sends a message beyond Canada.
item.dc:creator: Ian Austen
item.category: Charles III, King of the United Kingdom
item.media:credit: Adrian Wyld/The Canadian Press, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Prime Minister Mark Carney of Canada with the country’s new governor general, Louise Arbour, in Ottawa on Tuesday.

item.title: Trump Says U.S. Pausing Efforts to Guide Ships Through Strait Blocked by Iran
item.description: President Trump said a U.S. blockade of the Strait of Hormuz would “remain in full force.” The shift came after the defense secretary said U.S. efforts to free trapped vessels would continue.
item.dc:creator: Erica L. Green, Eric Schmitt, Greg Jaffe and Leo Sands
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: A billboard in Tehran showing the three supreme leaders of Iran last week, only one of whom is alive.

item.title: Iran announces a new system for traffic in the Strait of Hormuz.
item.dc:creator: Anushka Patil
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: A billboard in Tehran last week, depicting a closed Strait of Hormuz over the mouth of President Trump.

item.title: Can Trump Strong-Arm Iran?
item.description: The president seems to be searching for a silver bullet to end the war. There might not be one.
item.dc:creator: Katrin Bennhold
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: A billboard in Tehran on Saturday.

item.title: Andrew Tate’s Civil Trial Over Assault Claims Is Postponed in Britain
item.description: A British judge on Tuesday ordered a delay in a civil trial set for June after the police said they would reinvestigate criminal accusations against Mr. Tate.
item.dc:creator: Isabella Kwai
item.media:description: Andrew Tate speaking to reporters in Bucharest, Romania, in March 2025. The Police in Britain are reinvestigating criminal accusations against him.

item.title: Is It Time to Tax the Oil and Gas Industry’s Windfall?
item.description: The war in Iran has meant big profits for fossil fuel companies, prompting some lawmakers to call for a tax. But that would be easier said than done.
item.dc:creator: Claire Brown
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Gas prices in Washington on Friday.

item.title: What We Know About ‘Project Freedom’ in the Strait of Hormuz
item.description: President Trump on Tuesday abruptly paused an American initiative to help guide stranded ships through the Strait of Hormuz.
item.dc:creator: Amelia Nierenberg
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Ships in the Strait of Hormuz near Musandam, Oman, last week. Some 1,600 vessels are believed to be stranded.

item.title: Ford Says an Affordable Electric Pickup Truck is Still Coming Next Year
item.description: Ford Motor has written off $20 billion in electric vehicle investments but says it is forging ahead with an electric pickup that will sell for $30,000 next year.
item.dc:creator: Jack Ewing
item.category: Sports Utility Vehicles and Light Trucks
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Kate Medley for The New York Times
item.media:description: Recharging a Mustang Mach-E, Ford’s only electric vehicle right now.

item.title: The Venice Biennale and Its Many Flashpoints: An Explainer
item.description: The sudden death of a curator, the surprise participation of Russia and an unconventional U.S. entry are heating up the art world Olympics, which begins previews Tuesday.
item.dc:creator: Zachary Small
item.category: War Crimes, Genocide and Crimes Against Humanity
item.media:credit: Casey Kelbaugh for The New York Times
item.media:description: The central pavilion of the Giardini on Tuesday, first day of previews for the 61st Venice Biennale. The exhibition, “In Minor Keys,” was initiated by Koyo Kouoh and overseen by her team after her death last year.

item.title: Cruise Ship Struck by Hantavirus Is to Head to Canary Islands, W.H.O. Says
item.description: The Spanish government will receive the vessel. The World Health Organization said human-to-human transmission may have played a role in the outbreak.
item.dc:creator: Max Kim, Lynsey Chutel and Claire Moses
item.media:description: The MV Hondius moored off the coast of Cape Verde, an archipelago in the Atlantic Ocean, on Monday.

item.title: The Uncertain Fate of France’s Last Two Captive Orcas
item.description: France must decide what to do with two orcas marooned at a deserted marine park: move them to another park, or release them into an open-water sanctuary.
item.dc:creator: Ana Castelain
item.media:description: Keijo, an orca, in November at the Marineland park in France. Because he and his mother were born in captivity, experts say they would struggle in the open sea.

item.title: The Growing Rift Between the UAE and Saudi Arabia, Explained
item.description: Officials say competition between the countries is healthy. But tensions are mounting over energy quotas, regional conflicts and their different visions for the Middle East.
item.dc:creator: Ismaeel Naar
item.media:description: A photograph released by Saudi Royal Court showing Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman, right, and the Emirati leader, Sheikh Mohammed bin Zayed, in Riyadh, Saudi Arabia, in 2023.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Erica L. Green, Megan Mineiro, Eric Schmitt and Greg Jaffe

item.title: Fireworks Factory Explosion in China Kills at Least 26
item.description: Dozens more were injured in the explosion, which appeared to be one of the country’s deadliest in recent years.
item.dc:creator: Chris Buckley
item.media:description: The site of an explosion at a fireworks factory in Liuyang, China, on Monday. China has improved its record on industrial accidents in recent years, but fireworks factories remain a vulnerability.

item.title: What a Bike Ride in Cape Town Taught Me About Apartheid
item.description: A New York Times reporter joined a group of cyclists on a route meant to break down Cape Town’s lingering racial and economic barriers.
item.dc:creator: John Eligon and Tommy Trenchard
item.media:credit: Tommy Trenchard for The New York Times

item.title: Taiwan Could Learn From Ukraine. Informally, Connections Are Growing.
item.description: The governments have no official diplomatic or military ties. But a loose network led by company executives and volunteers is bridging some of that gulf.
item.dc:creator: Maria Varenikova and Amy Chang Chien
item.media:credit: Finbarr O’Reilly for The New York Times
item.media:description: Members of a Ukrainian drone regiment in the Kharkiv region in September. Taiwan is eager to learn from Ukraine’s experience, especially when it comes to drone warfare.

item.title: China’s Big Bet on Wind Power Is Paying Off
item.description: An industrial policy of subsidies and import restrictions laid the foundations for China to become almost as dominant in wind turbines as in solar panels.
item.dc:creator: Keith Bradsher and Qilai Shen
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: U.S. Revokes Visas of Board Members at Costa Rica’s Top Watchdog Newspaper
item.description: The move came after the paper critically covered the country’s president, who has cozied up to the United States. Analysts view the visa decision as part of a White House strategy to punish critics of its allies.
item.dc:creator: Emiliano Rodríguez Mega
item.media:credit: Mark Schiefelbein/Agence France-Presse, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Secretary of State Marco Rubio, left, with President Rodrigo Chaves of Costa Rica last year.

item.title: Tricked Into Fighting for Russia
item.description: Russia needs a lot of soldiers. Many Africans need work.
item.dc:creator: Katrin Bennhold
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Ed Ram for The New York Times
item.media:description: The outskirts of Nairobi, where Vincent Awiti used to live before he went to Russia.

item.title: American Commentator Who Worked for Russian TV Challenges Biden-era Charges
item.description: The Biden administration called Dimitri Simes, a former Trump adviser, a Russian propagandist. He says the charges against him violate free speech.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes
item.category: Russian Interference in 2016 US Elections and Ties to Trump Associates
item.media:description: Dimitri K. Simes, now living permanently in Moscow, said he believed that the Biden administration’s antipathy had its roots in the allegations that he had been involved in efforts by Russia to contact the Trump campaign.

item.title: A Portfolio of Work From The Times’s Pulitzer Prize-Winning Photographer
item.description: The photographer Saher Alghorra reported on the Gaza conflict while enduring the dire conditions his images portrayed.
item.media:credit: Saher Alghorra for The New York Times
item.media:description: Displaced Palestinians began returning to the north of Gaza after the Israeli military announced that a cease-fire was in effect.

item.title: U.S. Shot Down Iranian Missiles and Drones Aimed at American Vessels in Strait of Hormuz, Admiral Says
item.description: An exchange of fire threatened to shatter a fragile cease-fire as President Trump seeks to break Iran’s effective blockade of the waterway.
item.dc:creator: Eric Schmitt
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Ships and tankers in the Strait of Hormuz in April.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Aaron Boxerman, Ismaeel Naar and Eric Schmitt

item.title: Modi’s B.J.P. Wins West Bengal State Election for the First Time
item.description: The party of Prime Minister Narendra Modi took control of West Bengal for the first time and made gains around the country. Its defeated opponents accused it of cheating.
item.dc:creator: Alex Travelli, Hari Kumar and Pragati K.B.
item.media:description: Prime Minister Narendra Modi of India celebrating at the Bharatiya Janata Party headquarters in New Delhi on Monday after the party’s election victory in the state of West Bengal.

item.title: Leipzig, Germany, Car Ramming Attack Kills 2
item.description: Officials in Leipzig, where the attack occurred, said the driver was a 33-year-old German-born citizen. They have not yet determined his motive.
item.dc:creator: Christopher F. Schuetze and Sam Gurwitt
item.media:description: Emergency workers near the scene of a car-ramming attack in Leipzig, Germany, on Monday.

item.title: U.S. Warns China Over Iranian Oil as Sanctions Fight Intensifies
item.description: China told its independent refineries to disregard U.S. sanctions over their purchases of Iranian crude.
item.dc:creator: Alan Rappeport
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: The warning came from Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent, who is expected to participate in high-stakes meetings this month between the United States and China.

item.title: Mark Carney Pulls Canada Closer to Europe as Both Struggle With Trump
item.description: At a summit of European leaders, the Canadian prime minister was a special guest offering deals and friendship to jittery allies.
item.dc:creator: Matina Stevis-Gridneff
item.media:description: Prime Minister Mark Carney of Canada arriving for a meeting of the European Political Community on Monday in Yerevan, Armenia.

item.title: Europeans Prepare for a More Dangerous World in a Time of Economic Upheaval
item.description: A stalwart on defense spending, Poland is training its citizens in civil defense while balancing their jobs and families.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Cohen
item.media:credit: Maciek Nabrdalik for The New York Times
item.media:description: Polish civilians gathered at the headquarters of the 13th Silesian Territorial Defense Brigade in southern Poland for emergency preparedness training.

item.title: Germans Are Not Panicking Over Trump’s Troop Threats
item.description: Top leaders believe losing 5,000 U.S. troops will not hurt German security. But analysts fear an economic hit, especially in military communities.
item.dc:creator: Jim Tankersley and Christopher F. Schuetze
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Soldiers at a U.S. training facility in Hohenfels, southern Germany, last month.

item.title: Monster Truck Drives Into Crowd in Colombia, Killing 3
item.description: The driver of the truck appeared to lose control of the vehicle after performing a stunt at an outdoor rally in Popayán.
item.dc:creator: Leo Sands
item.media:credit: Colombia’s National Police Press, via Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: A monster truck after an accident during a show in Popayán, Colombia, on Sunday.

item.title: Strikes in U.A.E., Oman and at Sea Strain Iran Truce to the Breaking Point
item.description: The drone and missile attacks were the first of their kind since the U.S.-Iranian cease-fire began last month. They coincided with reported clashes between American and Iranian vessels in the Strait of Hormuz.
item.dc:creator: Aaron Boxerman, Ismaeel Naar and Sanam Mahoozi
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Ships have been stranded in the Strait of Hormuz for weeks after Iran effectively blockaded the waterway at the start of the war with the United States and Israel.

item.title: Rubio to Visit Rome After Trump’s Feud With the Pope and Meloni
item.description: Secretary of State Marco Rubio’s trip will come after President Trump criticized Pope Leo XIV and then turned on Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni of Italy for defending the pontiff.
item.dc:creator: Elisabetta Povoledo
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Secretary of State Marco Rubio at the U.S. Embassy to the Holy See in Rome last year.

item.title: Drone Hits a Moscow High-Rise Days Before a Major Military Parade
item.description: Air defenses in the Russian capital were breached as Ukraine expands long-range strikes.
item.dc:creator: Nataliya Vasilyeva and Matina Stevis-Gridneff
item.media:description: Damage to a high-rise residential building after a drone attack in Moscow on Monday.
